Homogeneous catalysts (same phase as reactants, e.g., HвЃє(aq) in ester hydrolysis) and heterogeneous (different phase, e.g., Pt in catalytic converters) both work by stabilizing transition states or providing easier intermediates. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely shows two PE diagrams (catalyzed vs uncatalyzed) and asks students to identify which is lower.

questions.prompt: Type of catalyst that is in the same phase as the reactants.
questions.explanation: Homogeneous catalysts share the same phase as the reactants (typically both dissolved in a solvent). Examples: HвЃє(aq) catalyzing ester hydrolysis, MnOв‚‚(aq) catalyzing Hв‚‚Oв‚‚ decomposition, transition-metal complexes (Wilkinson's catalyst for hydrogenation). They form intermediates that participate in the rate-determining step but are regenerated by the end. Advantages: easy mixing and uniform reactivity. Disadvantages: hard to separate catalyst from products. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely compares the energetics and PE diagrams of homogeneous vs heterogeneous catalysis.

questions.prompt: Type of catalyst in a different phase from reactants, like solid Pt with gas Hв‚‚.
questions.explanation: Heterogeneous catalysts exist in a different phase from reactants вЂ” typically solid catalysts with gas or liquid reactants. Examples: Pt-Pd-Rh in catalytic converters (oxidizes CO, HC; reduces NOx), Fe in Haber-Bosch ammonia synthesis (Nв‚‚ + 3 Hв‚‚ в†’ 2 NHв‚ѓ), Vв‚‚Oв‚… in the contact process (SOв‚‚ + ВЅOв‚‚ в†’ SOв‚ѓ, sulfuric-acid manufacture). Reactants adsorb to the catalyst surface, weakening bonds before product release. Surface-area maximization (nanoparticles, porous supports) is key. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely uses Haber-Bosch and catalytic converters as case studies.

questions.prompt: Biological catalyst вЂ” typically a protein вЂ” that accelerates a specific reaction.
questions.explanation: Enzymes are proteins (occasionally RNA вЂ” ribozymes) that catalyze biological reactions by lowering Ea, often by factors of 10вЃ¶ to 10В№ВІ over uncatalyzed rates. They have specific 3D active sites that bind substrates (lock-and-key or induced-fit models). Examples: catalase decomposes Hв‚‚Oв‚‚ at ~6 Г— 10вЃ¶ sвЃ»В№; carbonic anhydrase hydrates COв‚‚ at ~10вЃ¶ sвЃ»В№. Enzymes obey Michaelis-Menten kinetics (rate vs [S] gives V_max, K_M). AP Chemistry Unit 5 mentions enzymes as biological catalysts вЂ” typical free-response prompts compare enzyme efficiency to inorganic catalysts.

questions.prompt: Effect that adsorption on metal surface has on HвЂ“H bond before catalysis.
questions.explanation: When Hв‚‚ adsorbs onto a transition-metal surface (Pt, Pd, Ni), the H-H Пѓ bond partially overlaps with metal d-orbitals. Electron density flows into the H-H antibonding orbital, weakening (and eventually breaking) the H-H bond вЂ” the molecule dissociates into adsorbed H atoms. These atoms then migrate across the surface and react with a co-adsorbed substrate (e.g., ethene в†’ ethane in hydrogenation). The catalyst surface provides a low-Ea pathway through this dissociative chemisorption. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response on Haber-Bosch and hydrogenation reactions tests this surface-activation step.

questions.prompt: Quantity that catalysis does NOT change in a reversible reaction.
questions.explanation: Catalysts speed both forward and reverse reactions equally вЂ” they lower Ea_forward AND Ea_reverse by the same amount, so the ratio of rate constants k_f/k_r is unchanged. Since K_eq = k_f/k_r, the equilibrium constant is unaffected. Likewise, О”G, О”H, О”S, and final product yield at equilibrium are unchanged. Catalysts only let you reach equilibrium faster, never change where it lies. This is why Haber-Bosch uses high pressure (Le Chatelier's principle) to shift equilibrium right вЂ” Fe catalyst alone wouldn't help yield. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely traps students who claim catalysts shift K.

questions.prompt: Equation k = AВ·exp(-Ea/RT) linking rate constant to temperature.
questions.explanation: Svante Arrhenius (Nobel 1903) proposed k = AВ·e^(в€’Ea/RT) in 1889. A is the pre-exponential factor (collision frequency Г— steric factor, units of k), Ea is activation energy (J/mol), R = 8.314 J/(molВ·K), T in K. Higher T or lower Ea both increase k. The exponential term e^(в€’Ea/RT) is the fraction of collisions with E > Ea. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely asks students to (1) sketch Arrhenius plot ln k vs 1/T, slope = в€’Ea/R, (2) compute Ea from two (k, T) data points, or (3) compare A and Ea for catalyzed vs. uncatalyzed reactions.

questions.prompt: Proportionality constant k in a rate law; units depend on overall order.
questions.explanation: The rate constant k is the proportionality constant in a rate law. Its units depend on the overall reaction order: zero order в†’ M/s; first order в†’ 1/s; second order в†’ 1/(MВ·s); third order в†’ 1/(MВІВ·s). k is temperature-dependent (Arrhenius equation) but independent of concentration. The rate-law expression rate = k[A]^m[B]^n always gives rate in M/s (concentration over time), so unit balancing is a quick way to verify k's units. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely takes 1 point off for missing or wrong units on k вЂ” a recurring rubric trap.

questions.prompt: Pre-exponential factor A in Arrhenius captures these two physical quantities.
questions.explanation: The pre-exponential factor A combines (1) the rate at which reactant molecules collide (collision frequency Z, ~10В№В№ MвЃ»В№sвЃ»В№ at STP), and (2) the steric factor p (the fraction of collisions in which molecules are correctly oriented for reaction). A = ZВ·p. For simple atomic reactions p в‰€ 1; for complex molecules with strict geometric requirements (e.g., enzyme-substrate binding), p can be 10вЃ»вЃµ or smaller. A is roughly temperature-independent over short ranges. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response asks students to explain why two reactions with similar Ea can have different rates вЂ” A differences.

questions.prompt: Effect on k of raising T from 25 to 35 В°C if Ea is 50 kJ/mol.
questions.explanation: Plug into the two-T Arrhenius form: ln(kв‚‚/kв‚Ѓ) = в€’(Ea/R)(1/Tв‚‚ в€’ 1/Tв‚Ѓ). With Ea = 50,000 J/mol, Tв‚Ѓ = 298 K, Tв‚‚ = 308 K: ln(kв‚‚/kв‚Ѓ) = в€’(50000/8.314)(1/308 в€’ 1/298) = в€’6014 Г— (в€’1.09 Г— 10вЃ»вЃґ) = 0.655. kв‚‚/kв‚Ѓ = e^0.655 в‰€ 1.93, so approximately doubles. The textbook 'rate doubles with every 10В°C rise' approximation is rooted in this. For Ea в‰€ 80 kJ/mol the factor is ~3 over 10В°C; for Ea в‰€ 25 kJ/mol it is ~1.4. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response uses this Arrhenius algebra annually since 2014.

questions.prompt: Two-point form ln(kв‚‚/kв‚Ѓ) = -Ea/R В· (this) used for Arrhenius algebra.
questions.explanation: Subtracting two Arrhenius expressions gives ln(kв‚‚/kв‚Ѓ) = в€’(Ea/R) Г— (1/Tв‚‚ в€’ 1/Tв‚Ѓ). This is the workhorse equation for Ea calculations from two-temperature kinetics data. Equivalent forms: ln(kв‚‚/kв‚Ѓ) = (Ea/R) Г— (1/Tв‚Ѓ в€’ 1/Tв‚‚) and ln(kв‚Ѓ/kв‚‚) = в€’(Ea/R) Г— (1/Tв‚Ѓ в€’ 1/Tв‚‚) вЂ” sign matters. AP Chemistry's official equation sheet provides this form. Free-response routinely supplies (kв‚Ѓ, Tв‚Ѓ) and (kв‚‚, Tв‚‚), asks students to solve for Ea, and rubrics penalize unit errors (Ea in J/mol with R = 8.314, or Ea in kJ/mol with R = 8.314 Г— 10вЃ»Ві).

questions.prompt: Heat released or absorbed at constant pressure; symbol О”H.
questions.explanation: Enthalpy (H) is a state function: H = U + PV. At constant pressure, the change О”H = q_p (heat exchanged with surroundings). Exothermic reactions release heat (О”H < 0, products lower in energy); endothermic absorb heat (О”H > 0). Phase transitions: О”H_fusion (sв†’l, +6.01 kJ/mol for water), О”H_vap (lв†’g, +40.7 kJ/mol for water), О”H_sub (sв†’g). AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely tests О”H calculations from enthalpies of formation, Hess's law, bond enthalpies, and calorimetry вЂ” students must recognize which method fits the given data.

questions.prompt: О”H change for formation of 1 mol of compound from elements in standard states.
questions.explanation: Standard enthalpy of formation (О”HВ°_f) is the enthalpy change to make 1 mol of a compound from its elements in their standard states at 25В°C and 1 atm. By convention, О”HВ°_f for any element in its standard state is zero (Oв‚‚(g), Nв‚‚(g), graphite C, etc.). Examples: Hв‚‚O(l) = в€’285.8 kJ/mol; COв‚‚(g) = в€’393.5 kJ/mol; CHв‚„(g) = в€’74.8 kJ/mol. For any reaction, О”HВ°_rxn = ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(products) в€’ ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(reactants). AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely supplies a О”HВ°_f table and asks students to compute О”HВ°_rxn вЂ” a near-annual 2-point question.

questions.prompt: Heat input that breaks the C-H bond; bond breaking is always this.
questions.explanation: Bond breaking is always endothermic (О”H > 0); bond formation is always exothermic (О”H < 0). Average bond enthalpies are positive numbers representing the energy needed to break that bond. C-H в‰€ 413 kJ/mol; C-C в‰€ 347; C=C в‰€ 614; Cв‰ЎC в‰€ 839; Nв‰ЎN в‰€ 945. For any reaction: О”H в‰€ ОЈ(bonds broken in reactants) в€’ ОЈ(bonds formed in products) вЂ” both terms positive, with the difference giving О”H. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely tests bond-enthalpy calculations: students often forget the negative sign on the bonds-formed term and end up with the wrong sign for О”H.

questions.prompt: Estimate of О”H using the difference between bonds broken and bonds formed.
questions.explanation: О”H в‰€ ОЈ(bond enthalpies broken) в€’ ОЈ(bond enthalpies formed). This averaged-bond-enthalpy method gives approximate but useful О”H values. Example, combustion of methane: CHв‚„ + 2 Oв‚‚ в†’ COв‚‚ + 2 Hв‚‚O. Broken: 4 C-H (4 Г— 413) + 2 O=O (2 Г— 495) = 2642 kJ. Formed: 2 C=O (2 Г— 799) + 4 O-H (4 Г— 463) = 3450 kJ. О”H в‰€ 2642 в€’ 3450 = в€’808 kJ (exp. value в€’890 kJ). The discrepancy stems from using average bond enthalpies rather than molecule-specific values. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response asks for this estimate at least once per cycle.

questions.prompt: Standard О”SВ° calculated as ОЈ products' SВ° minus ОЈ reactants' SВ°.
questions.explanation: О”SВ°_rxn = ОЈОЅВ·SВ°(products) в€’ ОЈОЅВ·SВ°(reactants), analogous to enthalpy. Standard molar entropies SВ° are tabulated (in J/(molВ·K)) and вЂ” unlike О”HВ°_f вЂ” are NOT zero for elements (since S has an absolute zero, the third law sets S = 0 only at T = 0 K). Example: О”SВ° for Nв‚‚ + 3 Hв‚‚ в†’ 2 NHв‚ѓ = 2(192.5) в€’ [191.5 + 3(130.6)] = 385 в€’ 583.3 = в€’198.3 J/(molВ·K), matching the О”n_gas < 0 prediction. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely supplies an SВ° table and asks students to compute О”SВ°_rxn.

questions.prompt: Law stating О”H is a state function: total О”H is sum of step О”Hs.
questions.explanation: Germain Hess formulated his law of constant heat summation in 1840: the enthalpy change of a reaction is the same whether it proceeds in one step or many. This follows because H is a state function вЂ” О”H depends only on initial and final states, not path. To use Hess's law: write the target reaction; combine intermediate reactions (reverse them, multiply by integers as needed) so they sum to the target; sum the same operations on the О”H values. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response always tests Hess's law, often supplying 2-3 intermediate reactions and asking for the target О”H.

questions.prompt: Property of enthalpy that allows Hess's law to work: depends only on initial and final.
questions.explanation: A state function depends only on the current state of a system (T, P, n, composition), not on the path taken to reach that state. State functions: H, U, G, S, T, P, V вЂ” all uppercase variables. Path functions: q (heat), w (work) вЂ” depend on path. О”H of a reaction is independent of mechanism, catalyst use, or step decomposition; this is the foundation of Hess's law. О”H formation, combustion, atomization, etc. all combine additively when summing reactions. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response demands students articulate why Hess's law works: 'enthalpy is a state function.'.

questions.prompt: Sign change in О”H when a reaction is reversed.
questions.explanation: Reversing a chemical equation reverses the sign of its О”H: A в†’ B has О”H = x kJ/mol; B в†’ A has О”H = в€’x kJ/mol. Multiplying a reaction by a factor multiplies О”H by the same factor (doubling moles doubles enthalpy). These two operations вЂ” reversing and scaling вЂ” are the only two operations needed when applying Hess's law. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely supplies a few reactions and their О”H values, then asks students to combine them (with appropriate sign flips and integer multipliers) to produce the target reaction. Rubrics deduct 1 point per missed sign flip or scaling error.

questions.prompt: О”H calculated as ОЈ products' О”Hf minus ОЈ reactants' О”Hf.
questions.explanation: О”HВ°_rxn = ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(products) в€’ ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(reactants), where ОЅ is the stoichiometric coefficient. This is a special case of Hess's law derived by imagining: (1) decompose reactants to elements (cost: в€’ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(reactants)), (2) form products from elements (gain: +ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(products)). Adding gives the formula. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response is most likely to provide a О”HВ°_f table and ask students to compute О”HВ°_rxn вЂ” a near-annual fixture. Rubrics demand correct sign convention (products в€’ reactants), and any element in its standard state has О”HВ°_f = 0.

questions.prompt: Multi-step thermodynamic cycle that breaks lattice-formation into atomization + IE + EA + lattice.
questions.explanation: The Born-Haber cycle, developed by Max Born and Fritz Haber (~1919), uses Hess's law to extract lattice energy from measurable quantities. Steps: (1) atomization of metal (sublimation О”H_sub), (2) atomization of nonmetal (ВЅ bond enthalpy), (3) ionization of metal atoms (IE), (4) electron attachment to nonmetal atoms (в€’EA), (5) gas-phase ions combine into solid (в€’U, lattice energy). Sum equals О”HВ°_f of the ionic salt. Solving for U gives lattice energy magnitudes used in AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response for NaCl-class salts вЂ” students often must compute U given the other four О”Hs.

questions.prompt: Measure of dispersal of energy and matter in a system; symbol О”S.
questions.explanation: Entropy (S) measures the dispersal of energy (and matter) over the accessible microstates of a system. Ludwig Boltzmann gave S = k_B ln W (where W = number of microstates), engraved on his tombstone. Macroscopic features that raise S: more moles of gas, higher T, larger volume, more complex molecules, mixing. The second law of thermodynamics requires О”S_universe в‰Ґ 0 for any spontaneous process. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely asks students to predict the sign of О”S_rxn from inspection (О”n_gas, phase changes) вЂ” a near-annual 1-point question.

questions.prompt: Phase change that increases entropy: from this state to gas.
questions.explanation: Vaporization (liquid в†’ gas) sharply increases entropy because gas molecules have vastly more accessible volume and translational microstates than confined liquid molecules. For water, О”S_vap = +109 J/(molВ·K) at 25В°C. Fusion (s в†’ l) raises S by less: water's О”S_fus = +22 J/(molВ·K). The ranking S_gas в‰« S_liquid > S_solid is universal at any given T. AP Chemistry Unit 6 routinely asks students to predict О”S sign from phase changes вЂ” gas evolution в†’ О”S > 0; gas consumption в†’ О”S < 0; condensation, freezing в†’ О”S < 0. Quick-reference rubric: count gas moles change.

questions.prompt: Reaction with negative О”H; releases heat to surroundings.
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release heat to the surroundings, with О”H < 0. On a PE diagram, products sit below reactants. Examples: combustion (CHв‚„ + 2 Oв‚‚ в†’ COв‚‚ + 2 Hв‚‚O, О”H = в€’890 kJ/mol), neutralization, freezing, condensation. The surroundings warm up while the system loses energy. Note: exothermicity (favorable О”H) does NOT guarantee spontaneity вЂ” entropy and temperature also matter (О”G = О”H в€’ TО”S). AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely distinguishes thermodynamic favorability (О”G < 0) from exothermicity (О”H < 0) вЂ” students often conflate them and lose points.

questions.prompt: Temperature dependence: О”H < 0, О”S > 0 means spontaneous at this temperature.
questions.explanation: When О”H < 0 (exothermic) and О”S > 0 (entropy increases), then О”G = О”H в€’ TО”S is negative at any positive temperature вЂ” both terms drive О”G more negative. Example: 2 Hв‚‚Oв‚‚(aq) в†’ 2 Hв‚‚O(l) + Oв‚‚(g), О”H < 0 and О”S > 0 (gas evolved). Spontaneous at all T. The opposite case (О”H > 0, О”S < 0) is never spontaneous at any T. The other two cases вЂ” mixed signs вЂ” have a critical T = О”H/О”S at which О”G = 0; spontaneity depends on whether T is above or below this critical value. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response tests all four sign combinations.

questions.prompt: Second law statement: this property of the universe never decreases in a spontaneous process.
questions.explanation: The second law of thermodynamics states О”S_universe = О”S_system + О”S_surroundings в‰Ґ 0 for any process; equality holds only for reversible processes. For a process at constant T and P, the surroundings receive heat q_surr = в€’О”H_sys (sign-flipped) and О”S_surr = в€’О”H_sys/T. Combining: TВ·О”S_universe = TВ·О”S_sys в€’ О”H_sys = в€’О”G_sys. So О”G < 0 в†” О”S_universe > 0 в†” process is spontaneous. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response demands students articulate this link: spontaneity is governed by О”G_sys, which is equivalent to О”S_universe.

questions.prompt: Equation О”G = О”H - TО”S predicting reaction spontaneity.
questions.explanation: Josiah Willard Gibbs introduced G = H в€’ TS in 1873. At constant T and P, О”G = О”H в€’ TО”S predicts spontaneity: О”G < 0 в†’ spontaneous; О”G > 0 в†’ nonspontaneous; О”G = 0 в†’ equilibrium. The four sign cases: О”H < 0, О”S > 0 в†’ always spontaneous; О”H > 0, О”S < 0 в†’ never spontaneous; О”H < 0, О”S < 0 в†’ spontaneous at low T; О”H > 0, О”S > 0 в†’ spontaneous at high T. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely classifies reactions into these four cases and computes the critical T = О”H/О”S where spontaneity flips.

questions.prompt: Sign of О”G for a spontaneous reaction.
questions.explanation: A spontaneous process at constant T, P has О”G < 0. This means the process can occur without input of external energy (though kinetics may still make it slow вЂ” thermodynamic favorability is not the same as kinetic feasibility). О”G = 0 indicates equilibrium; О”G > 0 means the reverse direction is spontaneous. О”G also equals the maximum non-expansion work the reaction can do (e.g., electrical work in a galvanic cell: О”G = в€’nFE_cell). AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely tests both the sign convention and the equilibrium link О”G = 0 в†” K_eq position.

questions.prompt: Sign of О”S for the reaction Nв‚‚ + 3 Hв‚‚ в†’ 2 NHв‚ѓ.
questions.explanation: Nв‚‚(g) + 3 Hв‚‚(g) в†’ 2 NHв‚ѓ(g) goes from 4 moles of gas on the left to 2 moles on the right. О”n_gas = 2 в€’ 4 = в€’2, so the system loses gas moles and О”S < 0. Specifically, О”SВ°_rxn в‰€ в€’198 J/(molВ·K). Combined with О”HВ°_rxn = в€’92 kJ/mol (exothermic), the Gibbs free energy О”GВ° = О”H в€’ TО”S becomes positive at high T вЂ” which is why Haber-Bosch must use moderate temperatures (~450В°C) to balance kinetics vs equilibrium yield. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely tests this Haber-Bosch О”S/О”G analysis.

questions.prompt: Type of calorimeter used to measure О”H at constant pressure, like a Styrofoam cup.
questions.explanation: A coffee-cup (constant-pressure) calorimeter is a Styrofoam cup with a lid and thermometer, used for solution reactions and neutralizations. Open to atmosphere, so P is constant. q_p = О”H directly. Energy balance: heat released by reaction = heat absorbed by water (and the cup, if non-negligible): q_rxn = в€’(m_water Г— c_water Г— О”T). For aqueous reactions, the solution itself is usually treated as water for mass and specific-heat purposes. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely uses coffee-cup data: students compute q, divide by moles of limiting reactant, and report О”H in kJ/mol вЂ” with correct sign (exo or endo).

questions.prompt: Equation linking standard free energy and equilibrium constant: О”GВ° = -RT this K.
questions.explanation: О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K is the bridge between thermodynamics and equilibrium. At 25В°C (T = 298 K, RT = 2.479 kJ/mol): K > 1 в†” О”GВ° < 0 в†” reaction spontaneous in forward direction at standard conditions; K < 1 в†” О”GВ° > 0; K = 1 в†” О”GВ° = 0. For non-standard conditions, О”G = О”GВ° + RT ln Q, where Q is the reaction quotient вЂ” and О”G = 0 at equilibrium (Q = K). AP Chemistry Unit 9 expands this with electrochemistry (О”GВ° = в€’nFEВ°_cell, so EВ° = (RT/nF) ln K). AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response uses О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K to convert between K and О”GВ° at least once a cycle.

questions.prompt: Experiment measuring heat absorbed or released using a known mass of water.
questions.explanation: Calorimetry measures heat (q) exchanged between a system and surroundings using q = mВ·cВ·О”T, where m is mass of substance absorbing heat (usually water in the calorimeter), c is specific heat capacity (4.184 J/(gВ·K) for water), and О”T is temperature change. Coffee-cup (constant P) calorimetry measures О”H; bomb (constant V) calorimetry measures О”U. AP Chemistry Unit 6 lab requires students to (1) measure m, (2) record T_initial and T_final, (3) compute q, (4) convert to molar О”H or О”U by dividing by moles of limiting reactant вЂ” a 4-point staple.

questions.prompt: Amount of heat needed to raise 1 g of substance by 1В°C.
questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity (c) is the heat needed to raise 1 g (or 1 kg) of a substance by 1В°C (or 1 K). Water has an unusually high c = 4.184 J/(gВ·K) due to hydrogen bonding вЂ” a key reason coastlines have moderate climates. Metals have low c (Cu: 0.385; Al: 0.897 J/(gВ·K)) so they heat up and cool down quickly. Heat capacity (uppercase C) is for the whole sample, not per gram. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely tests q = mcО”T, especially in metal-into-water experiments where m_metalВ·c_metalВ·О”T_metal = в€’m_waterВ·c_waterВ·О”T_water.

questions.prompt: Quantity О”G equals at equilibrium.
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, О”G = 0 because the system has no net driving force to shift in either direction. Setting О”G = О”H в€’ TО”S = 0 gives the equilibrium temperature T_eq = О”H/О”S for a phase transition or reaction. Example: water's normal boiling point (T_eq for vaporization at 1 atm) = О”H_vap/О”S_vap = 40,700 J/(mol) / 109 J/(molВ·K) = 373 K = 100В°C вЂ” exactly the boiling point. The link between О”G and the equilibrium constant K is О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K, expanded in Unit 9. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely tests this critical-temperature calculation.

questions.prompt: Closed, rigid calorimeter that measures heat at constant volume.
questions.explanation: A bomb calorimeter is a sealed, rigid steel container ('bomb') submerged in an insulated water bath. Used for combustion: sample plus excess Oв‚‚ ignite electrically, releasing heat into the water. Constant V means q_v = О”U (internal energy, not enthalpy directly). For most gas-phase reactions, О”U в‰€ О”H; for reactions with О”n_gas в‰  0, О”H = О”U + О”n_gasВ·RT. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely uses bomb calorimetry for food-energy and fuel-energy calculations, sometimes requiring the small О”U в†’ О”H correction.

questions.prompt: Equation that converts mass, c, and О”T into total heat absorbed.
questions.explanation: The fundamental calorimetry equation: q = m Г— c Г— О”T. q in joules, m in grams, c in J/(gВ·K), О”T in kelvins (or В°C, since it's a difference). Sign: q > 0 means heat absorbed (T rises); q < 0 means heat released (T drops). For two-body equilibration (cold metal into warm water): mв‚ЃВ·cв‚ЃВ·(T_f в€’ Tв‚Ѓ) + mв‚‚В·cв‚‚В·(T_f в€’ Tв‚‚) = 0 вЂ” solve for T_f. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely applies q = mcО”T for at least 2 of the 4 calorimetry questions per cycle; specific-heat-capacity calculation is the alternate form (c = q/(mВ·О”T)).

questions.prompt: Equilibrium constant expressed in molar concentrations.
questions.explanation: Kc is the equilibrium constant written in terms of molar concentrations: for aA + bB в‡Њ cC + dD, Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / ([A]^a[B]^b). Pure solids and pure liquids are excluded (activity = 1). Kc is dimensionless by convention (concentrations are divided by 1 M standard state). Kc depends only on T, not on initial concentrations. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely asks for the Kc expression (1 pt), then plugs in equilibrium concentrations to compute its value (1 pt). Common pitfalls: including solids/liquids, missing exponents, or using initial instead of equilibrium concentrations.

questions.prompt: Equilibrium constant for the ionization of a weak acid in water.
questions.explanation: Ka is the equilibrium constant for HA в‡Њ HвЃє + AвЃ» in aqueous solution: Ka = [HвЃє][AвЃ»]/[HA]. Small Ka = weak acid (HOAc Ka = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ, pKa = 4.74). Large Ka = stronger acid (HF Ka = 6.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃґ, pKa = 3.17). Strong acids (HCl, HNOв‚ѓ, Hв‚‚SOв‚„, HClOв‚„, HBr, HI) have Ka в‰« 1 (effectively complete dissociation). For a weak acid in pure water, x = [HвЃє] = в€љ(Ka Г— [HA]в‚Ђ) (small-x approximation). AP Chemistry Unit 7 (continued in Unit 8) routinely tests Ka calculations and pH-from-Ka problems.

questions.prompt: Equilibrium constant expressed in partial pressures for gaseous reactions.
questions.explanation: Kp uses partial pressures (typically in atm) for gases: aA(g) + bB(g) в‡Њ cC(g) + dD(g), Kp = (P_C)^c(P_D)^d / ((P_A)^a(P_B)^b). Relationship to Kc: Kp = Kc(RT)^О”n_gas, where R = 0.0821 LВ·atm/(molВ·K), T in K, О”n_gas = moles of gaseous products в€’ moles of gaseous reactants. If О”n_gas = 0 (e.g., Hв‚‚ + Iв‚‚ в‡Њ 2 HI), Kp = Kc numerically. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely asks students to (1) write Kp, (2) convert between Kp and Kc, or (3) compute partial pressures using Dalton's law and ideal-gas relationships.

questions.prompt: О”n_gas relationship: Kp = Kc(RT) raised to this power.
questions.explanation: Kp = Kc(RT)^О”n_gas, where О”n_gas = (moles of gaseous products) в€’ (moles of gaseous reactants). Examples: Hв‚‚(g) + Iв‚‚(g) в‡Њ 2 HI(g), О”n = 2 в€’ 2 = 0, so Kp = Kc. Nв‚‚(g) + 3 Hв‚‚(g) в‡Њ 2 NHв‚ѓ(g), О”n = 2 в€’ 4 = в€’2, so Kp = Kc(RT)вЃ»ВІ (Kp smaller than Kc by factor of (RT)ВІ в‰€ 600 at 25В°C). Nв‚‚Oв‚„(g) в‡Њ 2 NOв‚‚(g), О”n = +1, so Kp = Kc(RT) (Kp larger than Kc). AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely requires this conversion when given Kc and asked for Kp at a specified temperature.

questions.prompt: Forward and reverse rate constants k_f and k_r relate to K via this ratio.
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, forward rate = reverse rate. For an elementary reaction A в‡Њ B: k_f[A] = k_r[B], so [B]/[A] = k_f/k_r = K_eq. This rate-constant ratio holds for elementary reactions; for multi-step mechanisms, the relationship is more complex (each step has its own k_f/k_r). Catalysts increase both k_f and k_r equally вЂ” by lowering Ea for both directions вЂ” so they leave K unchanged. AP Chemistry Unit 7 expects students to know K = k_f/k_r and use it to explain why catalysts don't shift equilibrium position. Free-response routinely tests this conceptual point.

questions.prompt: I, C, E columns of a table tracking initial, change, and equilibrium concentrations.
questions.explanation: An ICE table organizes equilibrium concentrations by row: Initial (starting concentrations), Change (in terms of x, signed by stoichiometry), Equilibrium (sum of I and C). The setup: write reactants and products as column headers; row 1 is initial concentrations; row 2 is +x or в€’x scaled by coefficients; row 3 sums them. Plug into K expression to solve for x. Example: Nв‚‚Oв‚„ в‡Њ 2 NOв‚‚, start 1.00 M Nв‚‚Oв‚„, 0 NOв‚‚. Change в€’x, +2x. Equilibrium (1.00 в€’ x), 2x. K = (2x)ВІ/(1.00в€’x). AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response uses ICE tables in nearly every equilibrium problem.

questions.prompt: Concentration change for the gas A in 2 A в‡Њ B starting at [A] = 0.50 M (in terms of x).
questions.explanation: For 2 A в‡Њ B, the stoichiometry says 2 moles of A are consumed for every 1 mole of B produced. So in an ICE table, the Change row for A is в€’2x and for B is +x. Equilibrium [A] = 0.50 в€’ 2x; equilibrium [B] = x. The factor of 2 from the stoichiometric coefficient is the single most common source of errors on AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response вЂ” students often write в€’x instead of в€’2x. The K expression then has K = x / (0.50 в€’ 2x)ВІ, not x / (0.50 в€’ x)ВІ. Always carry the coefficient into the Change row.

questions.prompt: Approximation that simplifies (0.10 в€’ x) в‰€ 0.10 when K is this small.
questions.explanation: When K is very small (K < ~10вЃ»вЃґ for an initial concentration ~0.1 M), the change x is small enough that (initial в€’ x) в‰€ initial to ~5% precision. This 'small x approximation' simplifies the quadratic from the K expression to a one-step calculation. After solving for x, always check: if x/initial > 5%, the approximation is invalid and you must use the quadratic formula. Example: HOAc Ka = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ; in 0.10 M, x в‰€ в€љ(Ka Г— 0.10) в‰€ 1.3 Г— 10вЃ»Ві, x/initial в‰€ 1.3% вЂ” approximation valid. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely awards 1 point for justifying the approximation.

questions.prompt: Quadratic-formula coefficients a, b, c plug into x = (-b В± в€љ(bВІ-4ac))/this.
questions.explanation: When the small-x approximation fails (K not small enough), the K expression yields a quadratic axВІ + bx + c = 0, solved by x = [в€’b В± в€љ(bВІ в€’ 4ac)] / (2a). For AP Chemistry Unit 7 ICE problems, typically only one root is physically meaningful (positive value < initial concentration). Example: Kc = 1.0 for Nв‚‚Oв‚„ в‡Њ 2 NOв‚‚ starting at 1.0 M Nв‚‚Oв‚„: (2x)ВІ / (1в€’x) = 1.0 в†’ 4xВІ + x в€’ 1 = 0, x = [в€’1 В± в€љ17]/8, take positive root x в‰€ 0.39 M. AP Chemistry permits calculators for the quadratic on free response; students who forget '2a' lose half the answer.

questions.prompt: Concentration to plug into the K expression: I, C, or E values?
questions.explanation: K is a function of EQUILIBRIUM concentrations, not initial. The E row of the ICE table contains the values to plug into Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b. Common ICE-table error: plugging initial concentrations directly into K (ignoring the change). Another: plugging the change (x) directly without computing initial в€’ x or initial + x. The structure is rigid: I в†’ C в†’ E, then E в†’ K. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response rubrics award separate points for (1) correct K expression, (2) correct ICE setup, (3) correct algebra, (4) correct final answer with units.

questions.prompt: Principle stating a system at equilibrium shifts to counteract any imposed stress.
questions.explanation: Henry Le Chatelier (1884) stated: when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that counteracts the disturbance. Stresses include adding or removing reactant or product, changing volume (changes partial pressures), and changing temperature. Catalysts are NOT a Le Chatelier stress вЂ” they don't change the equilibrium position, only how quickly it is reached. Adding inert gas at constant V also doesn't shift equilibrium (no partial-pressure change). AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely asks students to apply Le Chatelier's principle to predict shift direction вЂ” a near-annual 2-point question.

questions.prompt: Direction of shift when more reactant is added: shifts toward this.
questions.explanation: Adding more of any reactant raises Q below K в†’ reaction shifts forward (toward more products) to restore equilibrium. Removing reactant raises Q above K в†’ shifts reverse. The same logic applies to products: adding shifts reverse, removing shifts forward. Quantitative version: compute Q, compare to K, predict direction. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response demands a Le Chatelier explanation paired with a clear 'shifts right/forward toward products' or 'shifts left/reverse toward reactants' answer. Including the underlying Q vs K logic earns full credit; the principle name alone earns partial credit.

questions.prompt: Effect of increasing temperature on K for an endothermic reaction.
questions.explanation: Temperature is the ONLY stress that changes the value of K. For an endothermic reaction (О”H > 0), increasing T shifts equilibrium toward products (heat is a 'reactant' being added), so K increases. For exothermic (О”H < 0), increasing T shifts reverse and K decreases. Mathematically: О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K = О”HВ° в€’ TО”SВ°, so ln K = в€’О”HВ°/(RT) + О”SВ°/R. Plot ln K vs 1/T gives slope = в€’О”HВ°/R (van't Hoff plot). AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely tests the T-K relationship and asks students to identify О”H sign from a K-vs-T trend.

questions.prompt: Direction of shift for Nв‚‚ + 3 Hв‚‚ в‡Њ 2 NHв‚ѓ when total volume is decreased.
questions.explanation: Decreasing volume raises all partial pressures. The system shifts toward the side with FEWER moles of gas to relieve the pressure increase. For Nв‚‚ + 3 Hв‚‚ в‡Њ 2 NHв‚ѓ: 4 moles gas left, 2 moles right. Shifting right reduces total moles of gas (4 в†’ 2) в†’ lowers total pressure. So decreasing V shifts equilibrium toward products (NHв‚ѓ). This is the engineering basis for Haber-Bosch (operate at 200-300 atm for ~30% conversion). For reactions with equal gas moles (О”n_gas = 0, e.g., Hв‚‚ + Iв‚‚ в‡Њ 2 HI), volume changes have no effect. AP Chemistry Unit 7 tests this.

questions.prompt: Process that adds gas at constant V: noble-gas addition has this effect on equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Adding inert gas at constant volume does NOT shift the equilibrium of a gaseous reaction. While total pressure rises (Dalton's law), the partial pressures of the reacting species (which appear in Kp) are unchanged because their concentrations (mol/V) are unchanged. Therefore Q is unchanged and equilibrium does not shift. Compare to: adding inert gas at constant TOTAL pressure (which requires increasing V) DOES shift equilibrium вЂ” toward the side with more gas moles, because the diluted partial pressures favor the higher-mole side. AP Chemistry Unit 7 routinely tests this nuanced distinction.

questions.prompt: Equilibrium constant for dissolution of a slightly soluble salt.
questions.explanation: Ksp (solubility product) is the equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a slightly soluble ionic salt: M_aX_b(s) в‡Њ aM^(+)(aq) + bX^(в€’)(aq), with Ksp = [M^+]^a[X^в€’]^b. The solid is excluded (activity = 1). Small Ksp = very insoluble. Examples: AgCl Ksp = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ°; PbIв‚‚ Ksp = 7.1 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ№; CaFв‚‚ Ksp = 3.9 Г— 10вЃ»В№В№; BaSOв‚„ Ksp = 1.1 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ°. Solubility (s, in M) connects to Ksp via the stoichiometry: for AgCl, s = в€љKsp; for PbIв‚‚, Ksp = 4sВі. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely tests Ksp-to-solubility conversion.

questions.prompt: Effect on solubility of a salt when one of its ions is already present in solution.
questions.explanation: The common-ion effect reduces a salt's solubility when a soluble salt sharing one of its ions is added. Example: AgCl solubility in pure water (~1.34 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ M) drops to ~1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ№ M in 0.10 M NaCl, because the elevated [ClвЃ»] from NaCl forces the dissolution equilibrium left (Le Chatelier). Applied widely: fluoride-treated water inhibits hydroxyapatite dissolution in teeth; phosphate buffers control solubility in biology. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely tests common-ion calculations: solve an ICE table where initial [common ion] в‰  0, often using the small-x approximation.

questions.prompt: Ratio Q with non-equilibrium concentrations; compared to K to predict direction.
questions.explanation: The reaction quotient Q has the same algebraic form as K but uses arbitrary (often initial) concentrations. Compare Q to K: Q < K в†’ reaction proceeds forward (toward more products); Q > K в†’ reaction proceeds reverse (toward more reactants); Q = K в†’ already at equilibrium. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely gives initial concentrations and K, then asks students to (1) calculate Q, (2) compare Q to K, and (3) predict the direction of shift вЂ” a 3-point sequence. The reasoning is identical for Qc/Kc, Qp/Kp, Qsp/Ksp (Unit 7 solubility) and Qsp comparison for precipitation.

questions.prompt: Molar solubility of AgCl in water given Ksp = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ°.
questions.explanation: For AgCl(s) в‡Њ AgвЃє + ClвЃ» with 1:1 stoichiometry, [AgвЃє] = [ClвЃ»] = s (molar solubility). Ksp = s Г— s = sВІ. So s = в€љKsp = в€љ(1.8 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ°) = 1.34 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ M в‰€ 1.3 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ M. In grams per liter: s Г— M(AgCl) = 1.34 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ Г— 143.3 g/mol в‰€ 1.9 Г— 10вЃ»Ві g/L = 1.9 mg/L. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely asks for molar solubility from Ksp вЂ” and vice versa. For non-1:1 salts (PbIв‚‚, CaFв‚‚), the math is Ksp = 4sВі or Ksp = 27sвЃґ, etc., depending on stoichiometry.

questions.prompt: Comparison of Qsp and Ksp that predicts a precipitate forms.
questions.explanation: To predict precipitation: compute Qsp using the actual ion concentrations after mixing. If Qsp > Ksp, the system is supersaturated and a precipitate forms until [ions]^stoich = Ksp. If Qsp = Ksp, the solution is saturated (equilibrium). If Qsp < Ksp, no precipitate forms (unsaturated). Critical step: AFTER mixing, ion concentrations are diluted вЂ” students often forget to recalculate concentrations using the combined volume. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely tests this: 'Will a precipitate form when 100 mL of 0.10 M Pb(NOв‚ѓ)в‚‚ is mixed with 200 mL of 0.20 M KI?' вЂ” must compute diluted [PbВІвЃє] and [IвЃ»] first.

questions.prompt: Equilibrium constant for the reaction of a weak base with water.
questions.explanation: Kb is the equilibrium constant for B + Hв‚‚O в‡Њ BHвЃє + OHвЃ» in aqueous solution: Kb = [BHвЃє][OHвЃ»]/[B]. Small Kb = weak base (NHв‚ѓ Kb = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ, pKb = 4.74). Strong bases (NaOH, KOH, alkali hydroxides) have Kb в‰« 1. For conjugate acid-base pairs: Ka Г— Kb = Kw = 10вЃ»В№вЃґ at 25В°C (so pKa + pKb = 14). This means a weak acid has a relatively strong conjugate base (acetate is a weak but non-negligible base) and vice versa. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely uses Ka Г— Kb = Kw to convert between the two.

questions.prompt: Product of Ka of a weak acid and Kb of its conjugate base.
questions.explanation: Ka(HA) Г— Kb(AвЃ») = Kw, the autoionization constant of water. At 25В°C, Kw = 1.0 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃґ, so pKa + pKb = 14. This relationship lets you compute Kb of a conjugate base given Ka of the acid (or vice versa). Example: HOAc Ka = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ; OAcвЃ» Kb = 1.0 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃґ / 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ = 5.6 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ° (a very weak base). Kw is also temperature-dependent: ~1.5 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃґ at 37В°C (body temperature), so neutral pH is slightly below 7. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely tests the Ka-Kb-Kw triangle.

questions.prompt: PH of pure water at 25 В°C.
questions.explanation: In pure water at 25В°C, [HвЃє] = [OHвЃ»] = в€љKw = в€љ(10вЃ»В№вЃґ) = 10вЃ»вЃ· M. pH = в€’log[HвЃє] = 7. At higher temperatures, Kw increases (water autoionizes more), so neutral pH < 7 вЂ” but the solution is still neutral because [HвЃє] still equals [OHвЃ»]. Acidic solutions: [HвЃє] > [OHвЃ»], pH < 7 at 25В°C. Basic: [HвЃє] < [OHвЃ»], pH > 7. pH + pOH = 14 at 25В°C (pH = 14 в€’ pOH). AP Chemistry Unit 7 (and Unit 8) free-response demands pH calculations from Ka, Kb, mol-of-acid/base, dilution, and buffer compositions вЂ” all rooted in these definitions.

questions.prompt: Effect on solubility of an insoluble hydroxide when pH is lowered.
questions.explanation: Insoluble hydroxides like Mg(OH)в‚‚ (Ksp = 5.6 Г— 10вЃ»В№ВІ) and Fe(OH)в‚ѓ (Ksp = 2.8 Г— 10вЃ»ВівЃ№) become more soluble in acid. Adding HвЃє consumes OHвЃ» (forming water), so Le Chatelier shifts the dissolution equilibrium right, dissolving more solid. Similarly, weak-acid anions (COв‚ѓВІвЃ», SВІвЃ», POв‚„ВівЃ») make their salts more soluble in acid because protonation of the anion lowers its free concentration. Strong-acid anions (ClвЃ», NOв‚ѓвЃ», ClOв‚„вЃ») show no pH effect. AP Chemistry Unit 7 routinely tests pH-dependent solubility, which connects directly to Unit 8 acid-base equilibria.

questions.prompt: Equilibrium description: weak acids do this incompletely in solution.
questions.explanation: Weak acids ionize (dissociate) only partially in solution вЂ” typically 1-5% in 0.1 M solutions вЂ” establishing an equilibrium HA в‡Њ HвЃє + AвЃ». Strong acids ionize completely (>99.9%); weak acids' ionization is limited by their Ka. Acetic acid (Ka = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ) at 0.10 M gives x в‰€ 1.3 Г— 10вЃ»Ві M, about 1.3% ionization. This partial ionization is what makes weak-acid solutions buffer-capable when mixed with their conjugate-base salt (Unit 8) вЂ” addition of strong acid is absorbed by the conjugate base; addition of strong base is absorbed by the weak acid. AP Chemistry Unit 7 frames this for Unit 8.

questions.prompt: Negative base-10 logarithm of hydrogen ion concentration.
questions.explanation: SГёren SГёrensen introduced pH in 1909 as pH = в€’log[HвЃє] (or more precisely, в€’log a_HвЃє, where a is activity). At 25В°C, pH < 7 is acidic, pH = 7 is neutral, pH > 7 is basic. Each pH unit change corresponds to a 10-fold change in [HвЃє]. Example: pH 3 has [HвЃє] = 10вЃ»Ві M, 1000Г— more acidic than pH 6. Pure water at 25В°C has pH = 7 ([HвЃє] = 10вЃ»вЃ· M). At body temperature (37В°C), Kw is larger and neutral pH в‰€ 6.8. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely demands pH calculations from initial concentrations, Ka or Kb, dilution, or buffer composition.

questions.prompt: Negative base-10 logarithm of hydroxide ion concentration; equals 14 - pH at 25В°C.
questions.explanation: POH = в€’log[OHвЃ»]. At 25В°C, pH + pOH = 14 (because Kw = [HвЃє][OHвЃ»] = 10вЃ»В№вЃґ). pOH < 7 is basic, pOH = 7 neutral, pOH > 7 acidic вЂ” opposite the pH scale. For 0.10 M NaOH (a strong base): [OHвЃ»] = 0.10 M, pOH = 1.0, pH = 13.0. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely uses the pH + pOH = 14 relationship to compute pH of basic solutions: find [OHвЃ»] from base concentration or Kb, take pOH, then subtract from 14 to get pH. Direct [HвЃє] calculation from Kw is the alternative.

questions.prompt: Hydrogen ion concentration in a solution with pH 4.0.
questions.explanation: PH = в€’log[HвЃє], so [HвЃє] = 10^(в€’pH). For pH = 4.0: [HвЃє] = 10вЃ»вЃґ M = 1.0 Г— 10вЃ»вЃґ M = 0.0001 M. To go from pH to [HвЃє]: invert the negative-log relationship. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely demands this conversion in either direction. Common decimal-pH values: pH 4.74 в†’ [HвЃє] = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ M (acetic acid Ka, useful for buffer problems); pH 9.26 в†’ [HвЃє] = 5.5 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ° M, [OHвЃ»] = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ M (ammonia Kb). Calculator: 10^(в€’pH) directly, no algebra needed.

questions.prompt: Auto-ionization equilibrium constant of water at 25В°C, with units molВІ/LВІ.
questions.explanation: Water autoionizes: Hв‚‚O + Hв‚‚O в‡Њ Hв‚ѓOвЃє + OHвЃ», with equilibrium constant Kw = [HвЃє][OHвЃ»] = 1.0 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃґ at 25В°C. The pure-water [HвЃє] = [OHвЃ»] = в€љKw = 10вЃ»вЃ· M, giving pH 7. Kw is highly temperature-dependent because the autoionization is endothermic (О”H в‰€ +56 kJ/mol): Kw rises to ~6.8 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃµ at 0В°C and ~1.5 Г— 10вЃ»В№Ві at 50В°C. So pure water at 50В°C has pH в‰€ 6.4 вЂ” still neutral, but pH < 7. AP Chemistry Unit 8 emphasizes Kw and its T-dependence as the foundation of all aqueous acid-base equilibria.

questions.prompt: Percent ionization of a weak acid: this property of [HA]в‚Ђ inverse-relates to it.
questions.explanation: Percent ionization of a weak acid = ([HвЃє]_eq / [HA]_0) Г— 100%. Using the small-x approximation, [HвЃє] = в€љ(Ka Г— [HA]_0), so % ionization = в€љ(Ka/[HA]_0) Г— 100%. So % ionization INCREASES (not decreases) as [HA]_0 decreases вЂ” a more dilute weak acid is a higher fraction ionized. Example: 1.0 M acetic acid is 0.42% ionized; 0.10 M is 1.3%; 0.010 M is 4.2%. This counterintuitive trend is a frequent AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response trap вЂ” students often assume ionization fraction is constant. The underlying reason is Le Chatelier: dilution shifts ionization equilibrium right.

questions.prompt: Six common acids that fully dissociate in water.
questions.explanation: The six common strong acids: HCl, HBr, HI (hydrohalic acids except HF, which is weak), HNOв‚ѓ, Hв‚‚SOв‚„ (first ionization only вЂ” Kaв‚‚ в‰€ 0.012), and HClOв‚„. Each ionizes >99.9% in water: HA в†’ HвЃє + AвЃ». Their conjugate bases (ClвЃ», BrвЃ», IвЃ», NOв‚ѓвЃ», HSOв‚„вЃ», ClOв‚„вЃ») are extremely weak вЂ” they have negligible basicity (no measurable Kb). AP Chemistry Unit 8 students must memorize this list вЂ” strong vs weak classification is the first step in every acid-base problem, and getting it wrong derails the entire calculation.

questions.prompt: Solution that resists pH changes when small amounts of strong acid or base are added.
questions.explanation: A buffer is a solution that resists pH change when small amounts of strong acid or base are added. Made by combining a weak acid (HA) and its conjugate base (AвЃ») in comparable concentrations вЂ” typically as a weak acid plus its salt (e.g., HOAc + NaOAc) or a weak base plus its salt (e.g., NHв‚ѓ + NHв‚„Cl). When strong acid is added, AвЃ» + HвЃє в†’ HA; when strong base is added, HA + OHвЃ» в†’ AвЃ» + Hв‚‚O. Both reactions are nearly complete (large equilibrium driving force), absorbing the added strong acid/base. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely tests buffer pH calculation and behavior.

questions.prompt: Common strong bases вЂ” alkali hydroxides and heavier alkaline earth hydroxides.
questions.explanation: Common strong bases: Group 1 hydroxides (LiOH, NaOH, KOH, RbOH, CsOH) and the heavier Group 2 hydroxides (Ca(OH)в‚‚, Sr(OH)в‚‚, Ba(OH)в‚‚) вЂ” these fully dissociate in water, releasing one or two OHвЃ» ions per formula unit. Be(OH)в‚‚ and Mg(OH)в‚‚ have limited solubility and are considered weak. Other strong bases: sodium amide (NaNHв‚‚), sodium hydride (NaH) вЂ” react completely with water to make NaOH and a gas. AP Chemistry Unit 8 expects students to recognize strong bases for pH calculations: pH = 14 в€’ pOH, with pOH = в€’log([OHвЃ»] Г— n_OH per formula unit).

questions.prompt: Acid with Ka < 1, ionizing only partially in water вЂ” like acetic acid.
questions.explanation: Weak acids have Ka < 1 (often << 1) and ionize only partially in water. Common examples: acetic acid (Ka = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ), HF (Ka = 6.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃґ), formic acid (Ka = 1.8 Г— 10вЃ»вЃґ), HCN (Ka = 6.2 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ°). Their conjugate bases are weak but non-negligible: acetate Kb = 5.6 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ°; cyanide Kb = 1.6 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ (cyanide is a stronger base than acetate, mirroring HCN being weaker than HOAc). AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely supplies a weak acid's Ka and initial concentration, then asks for equilibrium pH using an ICE table and the small-x approximation.

questions.prompt: Relative strength comparison: the conjugate base of a stronger acid is this.
questions.explanation: The stronger the acid, the weaker its conjugate base (and vice versa). For strong acids (HCl, HNOв‚ѓ), conjugate bases (ClвЃ», NOв‚ѓвЃ») are essentially non-basic вЂ” they don't accept HвЃє from water in any measurable amount. For weak acids (HOAc, HCN), conjugate bases (OAcвЃ», CNвЃ») are weak but non-negligible bases. Quantitatively: Ka Г— Kb = Kw = 10вЃ»В№вЃґ. So a 'strong-acid weak-acid' continuum mirrors a 'weak-base strong-base' continuum on the conjugate side. AP Chemistry Unit 8 routinely tests this inverse relationship: 'identify the strongest conjugate base in the list' from a Ka table.

questions.prompt: Equation pH = pKa + log([AвЃ»]/[HA]) used for buffer calculations.
questions.explanation: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation pH = pKa + log([AвЃ»]/[HA]) вЂ” derived from Ka = [HвЃє][AвЃ»]/[HA] by taking в€’log вЂ” gives buffer pH in one step. Useful when [AвЃ»] and [HA] are both at least 100Г— the resulting [HвЃє] (typical buffer concentrations). The pH equals pKa when [AвЃ»] = [HA] (equal mole ratio). Effective buffering range: pH = pKa В± 1. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely uses Henderson-Hasselbalch to (1) compute buffer pH, (2) compute mole ratios needed to achieve a target pH, or (3) predict pH after adding strong acid/base.

questions.prompt: Ratio [AвЃ»]/[HA] that gives pH = pKa.
questions.explanation: When [AвЃ»] = [HA], the ratio [AвЃ»]/[HA] = 1, log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa + 0 = pKa. This is the 'half-equivalence point' on a titration curve and the center of a buffer's effective range. Buffers are most effective at pH в‰€ pKa because they have equal acid- and base-absorbing capacity. Example: an acetic acid/acetate buffer (pKa = 4.74) buffers best near pH 4.74; a phosphate buffer (pKaв‚‚ = 7.21 for Hв‚‚POв‚„вЃ»/HPOв‚„ВІвЃ») buffers near pH 7.2 вЂ” useful in biology. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely tests choosing the right pKa for a target pH.

questions.prompt: Quantity that measures how much strong acid or base a buffer can absorb.
questions.explanation: Buffer capacity is the amount of strong acid or base a buffer can absorb before its pH changes by more than ~1 unit. Capacity depends on (1) total concentration of buffer components (higher = more capacity) and (2) the [AвЃ»]/[HA] ratio (closest to 1:1 = highest symmetric capacity). A 1 M HOAc/1 M NaOAc buffer has 10Г— the capacity of a 0.1 M / 0.1 M buffer at the same pH. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely tests buffer-capacity reasoning: 'which of these two buffers will resist pH change better when 5 mL of 1 M HCl is added?' вЂ” students answer based on absolute concentration.

questions.prompt: Identity of the species present at pH 7.4 in a phosphate buffer.
questions.explanation: Hв‚ѓPOв‚„ has three pKa values: pKaв‚Ѓ = 2.15, pKaв‚‚ = 7.21, pKaв‚ѓ = 12.32. At physiological pH 7.4, [Hв‚‚POв‚„вЃ»]/[HPOв‚„ВІвЃ»] = 10^(7.21 в€’ 7.4) = 10^(в€’0.19) в‰€ 0.65, so HPOв‚„ВІвЃ» slightly dominates. This pair is the major intracellular buffer. Cells maintain ~10% phosphate by mass in mitochondria and bone hydroxyapatite. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response uses phosphate as the canonical 'buffer at pH ~7' system; students must recognize that pH 7.4 falls inside pKaв‚‚ В± 1, putting the buffer in the Hв‚‚POв‚„вЃ»/HPOв‚„ВІвЃ» region, not the Hв‚ѓPOв‚„/Hв‚‚POв‚„вЃ» region (pKaв‚Ѓ) or the HPOв‚„ВІвЃ»/POв‚„ВівЃ» region (pKaв‚ѓ).

questions.prompt: Common biological buffer pair operating near pH 7.4 in human blood.
questions.explanation: The carbonic acid / bicarbonate buffer (Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ / HCOв‚ѓвЃ», pKaв‚Ѓ в‰€ 6.35) is the most important blood buffer. Equilibrium: COв‚‚(aq) + Hв‚‚O в‡Њ Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ в‡Њ HвЃє + HCOв‚ѓвЃ». Blood pH is held at 7.4 В± 0.04. When HвЃє rises (acidosis), HCOв‚ѓвЃ» absorbs it; when OHвЃ» rises (alkalosis), Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ neutralizes it. The buffer is 'open' вЂ” COв‚‚ exchanges with the lungs, providing extra capacity beyond a closed buffer's. Failure of this system (e.g., respiratory acidosis from emphysema, metabolic acidosis from diabetic ketoacidosis) is medically serious. AP Chemistry Unit 8 connects buffers to biological pH homeostasis.

questions.prompt: Plot of pH versus volume of titrant added during a titration.
questions.explanation: A titration curve plots pH (y-axis) vs volume of titrant added (x-axis). Characteristic features: (1) initial pH (acid or base alone), (2) gradual rise/fall through buffer region (when titrating a weak acid/base), (3) steep rise/fall at equivalence point, (4) plateau as excess titrant accumulates. The shape depends on whether the analyte and titrant are strong or weak. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely shows a titration curve and asks students to (1) identify equivalence and half-equivalence points, (2) read pKa from the half-equivalence pH, and (3) match the curve to the analyte type.

questions.prompt: Curve point where moles of titrant added equals half the moles of analyte.
questions.explanation: The half-equivalence point in a weak-acid + strong-base titration is where exactly half of the weak acid has been converted to its conjugate base: [HA] = [AвЃ»]. By Henderson-Hasselbalch, pH = pKa. This is the most powerful diagnostic on a titration curve вЂ” read the half-equivalence pH directly off the y-axis, and that's the weak acid's pKa. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely uses this 'read pKa from the half-equivalence point' technique to identify an unknown weak acid. Note: there is NO half-equivalence-point shortcut for strong-acid titrations (no buffer region exists).

questions.prompt: Concentration relationship at the second equivalence point of Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ: most of the carbonate is in this form.
questions.explanation: Titration of a diprotic acid like Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ has two equivalence points. At the first eq pt, all Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ has been converted to HCOв‚ѓвЃ» (bicarbonate). At the second eq pt, all HCOв‚ѓвЃ» has been converted to COв‚ѓВІвЃ» (carbonate). Each equivalence point appears as a separate inflection on the titration curve. Between them, the two species coexist in a buffer system вЂ” useful for blood pH regulation. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely uses Hв‚ѓPOв‚„ titration as a case study: three Ka's, three equivalence points, two buffer plateaus where one pair (e.g., Hв‚‚POв‚„вЃ»/HPOв‚„ВІвЃ») is dominant.

questions.prompt: Color-change substance used to indicate equivalence in a titration.
questions.explanation: An indicator is a weak organic acid (HIn) or base whose protonated and deprotonated forms have different colors. Color change occurs near pH = pKa(In) В± 1. Phenolphthalein (pKa в‰€ 9.4) is colorless below pH 8 and pink above pH 10 вЂ” used for strong-acid + strong-base or weak-acid + strong-base titrations. Methyl orange (pKa в‰€ 3.7) is red below pH 3.1 and yellow above pH 4.4 вЂ” used for weak-base + strong-acid titrations. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response demands students choose an indicator whose color-change range straddles the equivalence pH, not arbitrarily.

questions.prompt: Region of a weak-acid titration where pH changes slowly with added base.
questions.explanation: The buffer region of a titration curve is the relatively flat portion where pH changes slowly with added titrant. It exists only for weak-acid + strong-base or weak-base + strong-acid titrations (no buffer region for strong+strong). The region extends roughly from 10% to 90% of the way to the equivalence point вЂ” about pH = pKa в€’ 1 to pH = pKa + 1. The half-equivalence point sits at the center, where pH = pKa. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely asks students to identify the buffer region on a curve, read the pKa from its midpoint, and explain why pH changes are minimal there.

questions.prompt: Acid that donates more than one proton in successive equilibria.
questions.explanation: A polyprotic acid donates more than one HвЃє in successive equilibria, each with its own Ka. Examples: Hв‚‚SOв‚„ (diprotic вЂ” Kaв‚Ѓ в‰€ 10ВІ strong; Kaв‚‚ = 1.2 Г— 10вЃ»ВІ medium-strong), Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ (carbonic, Kaв‚Ѓ = 4.3 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ·, Kaв‚‚ = 4.8 Г— 10вЃ»В№В№), Hв‚ѓPOв‚„ (triprotic, Kaв‚Ѓ = 7.5 Г— 10вЃ»Ві, Kaв‚‚ = 6.2 Г— 10вЃ»вЃё, Kaв‚ѓ = 4.8 Г— 10вЃ»В№Ві). Successive Ka values decrease (usually by 10вЃµ to 10вЃ·) because removing a proton from a negatively charged species is harder. AP Chemistry Unit 8 routinely tests polyprotic-acid pH calculations using only Kaв‚Ѓ when Kaв‚‚ << Kaв‚Ѓ.

questions.prompt: PH at the equivalence point for a strong acid + strong base titration.
questions.explanation: At the equivalence point of a strong-acid strong-base titration, moles of acid = moles of base, and the only species are water and a neutral salt (NaCl from HCl + NaOH). Salts of strong acids with strong bases don't hydrolyze, so pH = 7. For weak-acid + strong-base titrations, equivalence pH > 7 because the conjugate base (e.g., acetate) hydrolyzes water to give OHвЃ». For weak-base + strong-acid, equivalence pH < 7 (conjugate acid hydrolyzes). AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely tests equivalence-point pH prediction вЂ” and indicator selection based on that pH.

questions.prompt: Relationship between successive Ka values: Kaв‚‚ is much smaller than this.
questions.explanation: For any polyprotic acid, Kaв‚Ѓ >> Kaв‚‚ >> Kaв‚ѓ, with each successive Ka typically 10вЃµ-10вЃ· times smaller. The reason: removing HвЃє from a neutral Hв‚‚A is much easier than from a negatively charged HAвЃ» (and trivially from a doubly-negative AВІвЃ»). For Hв‚ѓPOв‚„, Kaв‚Ѓ/Kaв‚‚ в‰€ 10вЃµ, Kaв‚‚/Kaв‚ѓ в‰€ 10вЃµ. This large gap means most polyprotic-acid pH calculations only need to consider Kaв‚Ѓ вЂ” the second ionization contributes negligibly to [HвЃє]. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response simplifies polyprotic pH problems using this approximation, then notes when Kaв‚‚ becomes relevant (concentrated solutions, salt of HAвЃ»).

questions.prompt: PH of 0.10 M HCl.
questions.explanation: HCl is a strong acid вЂ” it ionizes 100% in solution: HCl в†’ HвЃє + ClвЃ». So [HвЃє] = 0.10 M, pH = в€’log(0.10) = 1.0. For any strong monoprotic acid (HCl, HNOв‚ѓ, HBr, HI, HClOв‚„), pH = в€’log(C_acid). For diprotic strong acids (Hв‚‚SOв‚„), the first HвЃє is essentially complete (giving [HвЃє] = C); the second HвЃє has Kaв‚‚ в‰€ 1.2 Г— 10вЃ»ВІ (still strong but not complete), so 0.10 M Hв‚‚SOв‚„ has pH в‰€ 0.85, not 0.7. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely tests strong-acid pH and contrasts with weak-acid pH (which always > pH of strong acid at the same concentration).

questions.prompt: Amphiprotic ion that can act as either acid or base вЂ” found in NaHCOв‚ѓ.
questions.explanation: HCOв‚ѓвЃ» is amphiprotic (amphoteric) вЂ” it can act as an acid (donating HвЃє to become COв‚ѓВІвЃ»; Kaв‚‚ of Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ = 4.8 Г— 10вЃ»В№В№) or as a base (accepting HвЃє to become Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ; Kb = Kw/Kaв‚Ѓ = 2.3 Г— 10вЃ»вЃё). Since Kb > Ka here, NaHCOв‚ѓ solutions are slightly basic (pH в‰€ 8.3). Other amphiprotic ions: Hв‚‚POв‚„вЃ», HPOв‚„ВІвЃ», HSOв‚„вЃ», Hв‚‚O itself. AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely tests amphiprotic-species recognition and pH prediction: pH в‰€ ВЅ(pKaв‚Ѓ + pKaв‚‚) for the intermediate species in a diprotic acid, regardless of starting concentration.

questions.prompt: Equation linking standard О”GВ° to equilibrium constant K via natural log.
questions.explanation: О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K connects thermodynamics (О”GВ°) and equilibrium (K). At 25В°C with R = 8.314 J/(molВ·K), this gives О”GВ° = в€’5.71 Г— log K kJ/mol (using log = ln/2.303). K > 1 в†” О”GВ° < 0 в†” products favored at standard state. K < 1 в†” О”GВ° > 0 в†” reactants favored. Larger |О”GВ°| means more extreme K. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely converts between K and О”GВ°, using this equation as a hinge. The corresponding non-standard equation О”G = О”GВ° + RT ln Q lets you predict spontaneity at any composition.

questions.prompt: Standard-state composition condition: all reactants and products at this activity.
questions.explanation: Standard state in chemistry: solutes at 1 M concentration, gases at 1 atm partial pressure, pure liquids/solids at unit activity, temperature usually specified (often 25В°C = 298 K). At standard state, the reaction quotient Q = 1 (log 1 = 0), so О”G = О”GВ° + RT ln Q simplifies to О”G = О”GВ°. Standard state is a reference point вЂ” real reactions rarely run at standard state, but О”GВ° is the conventional tabulated value. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely distinguishes О”GВ° (standard) from О”G (actual conditions), with the latter calculated using the Q correction.

questions.prompt: Order of activity: metals at the top of the activity series do this to those below.
questions.explanation: The activity series ranks metals by reducing strength (top = strongest reducer). A metal higher in the series will displace (reduce) the ions of any metal lower in the series. For example: Zn + CuВІвЃє в†’ ZnВІвЃє + Cu proceeds because Zn is higher than Cu; Cu + ZnВІвЃє в†’ no reaction. Activity series order (selected metals): Li > K > Ba > Ca > Na > Mg > Al > Mn > Zn > Cr > Fe > Cd > Ni > Sn > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Hg > Pt > Au. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely tests predictions of single-displacement reactions using this series.

questions.prompt: Relationship: О”GВ° = -nFEВ°cell links thermodynamics and electrochemistry.
questions.explanation: О”GВ° = в€’nFEВ°_cell relates standard cell potential to standard Gibbs energy. Here n is moles of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, and F = 96,485 C/mol is Faraday's constant. Spontaneous cells (EВ°_cell > 0) have О”GВ° < 0; non-spontaneous (electrolytic) cells (EВ°_cell < 0) have О”GВ° > 0. Connecting all three: О”GВ° = в€’nFEВ°_cell = в€’RT ln K, so EВ°_cell = (RT/nF) ln K. At 25В°C, EВ°_cell = (0.0592/n) Г— log K. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely converts between all four quantities (О”GВ°, EВ°_cell, K, О”HВ°) вЂ” the most-tested computational chain in Unit 9.

questions.prompt: Sign of О”GВ° vs Q comparison: О”G < 0 means Q is less than this.
questions.explanation: О”G = О”GВ° + RT ln Q = RT ln(Q/K). So О”G < 0 (spontaneous forward) в†” Q < K (reaction must proceed forward to reach equilibrium). О”G > 0 (spontaneous reverse) в†” Q > K. О”G = 0 в†” Q = K (at equilibrium). This Q-vs-K comparison is the same as Le Chatelier's prediction but stated thermodynamically. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely supplies Q values (computed from current concentrations) and asks students to predict reaction direction and the sign of О”G вЂ” the Q < K в†’ forward в†’ О”G < 0 chain is a high-yield rubric point.

questions.prompt: Spontaneous electrochemical cell that converts chemical energy to electrical work.
questions.explanation: A galvanic (or voltaic) cell exploits a spontaneous redox reaction to generate electrical work. Named for Luigi Galvani (frog-leg twitches, 1780) and Alessandro Volta (first battery, 1800). Components: two half-cells (each with an electrode in an electrolyte solution), a salt bridge or porous membrane (lets ions flow to maintain charge neutrality), and an external wire (lets electrons flow). The Daniell cell (Zn/ZnВІвЃє || CuВІвЃє/Cu) is the textbook example, EВ°_cell = +1.10 V. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely tests cell-construction details, electrode assignments, and current/voltage measurements.

questions.prompt: Electrode where oxidation occurs.
questions.explanation: The anode is the electrode where oxidation occurs ('AN OX' mnemonic). In a galvanic cell, the anode is the negative terminal because electrons flow out of it into the external circuit; in an electrolytic cell, the anode is positive (driven by external power). Mass of the anode decreases over time as the metal is oxidized to ions (e.g., Zn в†’ ZnВІвЃє + 2 eвЃ»). AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely identifies the anode from given half-reactions, computes mass loss using Faraday's law (m = ItM/nF), and traces the electron flow direction in cell diagrams.

questions.prompt: Electrode where reduction occurs.
questions.explanation: The cathode is the electrode where reduction occurs ('RED CAT' mnemonic). In a galvanic cell, cathode is positive (electrons flow into it from the external circuit); in an electrolytic cell, cathode is negative. Mass of the cathode usually INCREASES as cations from solution are reduced and plated as solid metal (e.g., CuВІвЃє + 2 eвЃ» в†’ Cu). AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely uses Faraday's law m = ItM/(nF) to compute mass plated at the cathode given current and time вЂ” a near-annual calculation.

questions.prompt: Effect on E_cell when [products] is increased above standard 1 M.
questions.explanation: Nernst: E = EВ° в€’ (0.0592/n) log Q. Increasing [products] raises Q above 1 (log Q > 0), so the (0.0592/n) log Q term is positive, subtracting from EВ° and lowering E. Conversely, decreasing [products] or increasing [reactants] lowers Q below 1 (log Q < 0), so E rises above EВ°. At Q = K (equilibrium), E = 0 (cell is dead). AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely uses this to explain battery 'going flat' and concentration-cell behavior: two half-cells with the same identity but different concentrations spontaneously equilibrate, generating voltage as the higher concentration is reduced and the lower is oxidized.

questions.prompt: Salt bridge function: provides ions to balance these as electrons move externally.
questions.explanation: A salt bridge (a U-tube containing inert electrolyte like KCl or KNOв‚ѓ in agar) connects the two half-cells and lets ions flow between them to maintain charge neutrality. As ZnВІвЃє accumulates in the anode compartment (from Zn в†’ ZnВІвЃє + 2eвЃ»), anions (ClвЃ» or NOв‚ѓвЃ») migrate from the salt bridge into that compartment. As CuВІвЃє is depleted at the cathode (CuВІвЃє + 2eвЃ» в†’ Cu), cations (KвЃє) migrate from the salt bridge into that compartment. Without a salt bridge, charges would build up and the cell would stop almost immediately. AP Chemistry Unit 9 demands students explain salt-bridge function with ionic-migration directions.

questions.prompt: Reference electrode whose reduction potential is defined as 0.00 V.
questions.explanation: The standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), 2 HвЃє(aq, 1 M) + 2 eвЃ» в‡Њ Hв‚‚(g, 1 atm), is defined as EВ° = 0.00 V at 25В°C. It consists of a Pt-foil electrode coated with platinum black, in 1 M HCl, with Hв‚‚ gas bubbled over it at 1 atm. All other standard reduction potentials are measured relative to SHE. SHE is impractical for routine use, so secondary references (saturated calomel electrode, Ag/AgCl, +0.197 V vs SHE) are preferred in modern labs. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely defines SHE as the zero-point reference.

questions.prompt: Positive standard reduction potential: this species is a strong this kind of agent.
questions.explanation: A large positive EВ°_red means the species has a strong pull on electrons вЂ” it is reduced readily and therefore is a strong oxidizing agent. Examples: Fв‚‚/FвЃ» EВ° = +2.87 V (strongest common oxidizer), MnOв‚„вЃ»/MnВІвЃє EВ° = +1.51 V (acidic), Clв‚‚/ClвЃ» EВ° = +1.36 V. Conversely, a large negative EВ°_red means the species is hard to reduce (rather, its reduced form is easily oxidized вЂ” a strong reducing agent): LiвЃє/Li EВ° = в€’3.04 V, KвЃє/K EВ° = в€’2.93 V. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely ranks species by reducing/oxidizing strength using a standard-potential table.

questions.prompt: Sign of О”GВ° when K > 1.
questions.explanation: О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K. When K > 1, ln K > 0, so в€’RT ln K < 0, meaning О”GВ° < 0. At 25В°C: K = 10 в†’ О”GВ° = в€’5.71 kJ/mol; K = 1000 в†’ О”GВ° = в€’17.1 kJ/mol; K = 10вЃ»Ві в†’ О”GВ° = +17.1 kJ/mol. So О”GВ° measures the thermodynamic 'pull' toward products at standard conditions. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely supplies K (or Ksp) and asks for О”GВ° (or vice versa), as well as testing the sign reasoning: 'is О”GВ° positive or negative for this dissociation with Ka = 10вЃ»вЃµ?' Answer: positive (since K < 1).

questions.prompt: Standard cell potential = EВ°(cathode) в€’ this.
questions.explanation: Standard cell potential EВ°_cell = EВ°_cathode в€’ EВ°_anode, where both EВ° values are standard reduction potentials (always tabulated as reductions, never as oxidations). For Daniell cell (CuВІвЃє/Cu cathode = +0.34 V; ZnВІвЃє/Zn anode = в€’0.76 V): EВ°_cell = 0.34 в€’ (в€’0.76) = +1.10 V. A positive EВ°_cell means the cell is spontaneous (galvanic). To convert О”GВ°: О”GВ° = в€’nFEВ°_cell, where n is moles of electrons transferred and F = 96485 C/mol. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely computes both EВ°_cell and О”GВ°.

questions.prompt: Cell with two identical half-reactions but different ion concentrations.
questions.explanation: A concentration cell has both half-cells with the same redox couple (e.g., CuВІвЃє/Cu vs CuВІвЃє/Cu) but different ion concentrations. Standard cell potential EВ°_cell = 0 (since both half-cells have the same EВ°), so all voltage comes from the Nernst Q term: E_cell = в€’(0.0592/n) log([dilute]/[concentrated]). The dilute compartment serves as the anode (oxidation, increasing [CuВІвЃє]); the concentrated compartment is the cathode (reduction, decreasing [CuВІвЃє]). Useful for measuring ion concentrations (ion-selective electrodes work this way). AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely tests concentration-cell calculation and direction of electron flow.

questions.prompt: Equation that gives non-standard cell potential at any concentration.
questions.explanation: Walther Nernst (Nobel 1920) derived E = EВ° в€’ (RT/nF) ln Q in 1889. At 25В°C, this simplifies to E = EВ° в€’ (0.0592/n) log Q, where Q is the reaction quotient. As products accumulate (Q rises), E drops; as reactants are consumed, the cell potential decreases toward zero (Q = K, equilibrium, dead battery). AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely uses the Nernst equation to (1) compute E at non-standard concentrations, (2) explain why a galvanic cell stops working when Q reaches K, or (3) predict the effect of changing concentrations on cell voltage.

questions.prompt: At 25 В°C, Nernst constant 0.0592 has these units.
questions.explanation: At 25В°C, RT/F = 8.314 Г— 298 / 96485 = 0.0257 V. Converting from ln to log: (RT/F) Г— ln(10) = 0.0257 Г— 2.303 = 0.0592 V. So the simplified Nernst form is E = EВ° в€’ (0.0592 V / n) Г— log Q. The 0.0592 V coefficient is widely memorized. At body temperature (37В°C, 310 K), the constant rises slightly to ~0.0615 V. AP Chemistry's official equation sheet provides 0.0592 V form for the simplified Nernst вЂ” students using either ln or log form is acceptable as long as the prefactor matches.

questions.prompt: Equation О”G = О”GВ° + RT ln Q used at non-standard conditions.
questions.explanation: О”G = О”GВ° + RT ln Q gives the Gibbs energy at any composition. At equilibrium, Q = K and О”G = 0, which recovers О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K. О”G (not О”GВ°) determines actual spontaneity at the current state вЂ” a reaction may have О”GВ° > 0 but still proceed forward if Q is much less than K (so RT ln Q is sufficiently negative to make total О”G negative). AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response uses this equation to evaluate reactions at non-standard conditions, often in electrochemistry contexts where the Nernst equation does the analogous work for cell potentials.

questions.prompt: Number of moles of electrons transferred per mole of CuВІвЃє reduced to Cu.
questions.explanation: CuВІвЃє + 2 eвЃ» в†’ Cu has n = 2 electrons per copper. The same applies for ZnВІвЃє/Zn (n = 2). For AgвЃє + eвЃ» в†’ Ag, n = 1; for AlВівЃє + 3 eвЃ» в†’ Al, n = 3. n appears in О”GВ° = в€’nFEВ°_cell (so n changes the magnitude but not sign of О”GВ°), in Faraday's law m = ItM/(nF), and in the Nernst equation E = EВ° в€’ (RT/nF) ln Q. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely traps students who use the wrong n: must be CONSISTENT moles of electrons transferred in the BALANCED overall cell reaction, not just one half-cell вЂ” for Daniell cell, n = 2, balancing 1 Zn в†” 1 CuВІвЃє.

questions.prompt: Reading on a voltmeter at the end of a battery's useful lifetime.
questions.explanation: A 'dead' battery has reached Q = K, the equilibrium state where the redox reaction has no more thermodynamic driving force. By the Nernst equation, E = EВ° в€’ (0.0592/n) log Q. When Q = K, log Q = log K = в€’(n/0.0592) Г— EВ°, so E = EВ° в€’ EВ° = 0. No voltage, no current вЂ” the cell is at equilibrium. In real batteries, internal resistance and side reactions also degrade performance, but the thermodynamic limit is always E = 0 at Q = K. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely asks students to explain WHY a battery dies (Q в†’ K в†’ E в†’ 0), using Nernst reasoning.

questions.prompt: Cell that uses external electrical power to drive a non-spontaneous redox reaction.
questions.explanation: An electrolytic cell uses external electrical power to drive a non-spontaneous redox reaction (EВ°_cell < 0, О”GВ° > 0). Industrial uses: aluminum production from Alв‚‚Oв‚ѓ in the Hall-HГ©roult process (1886); chlor-alkali production of Clв‚‚ + NaOH from brine; electroplating; electrorefining of copper. The polarity is reversed from a galvanic cell: anode is positive (oxidation forced), cathode is negative (reduction forced). AP Chemistry Unit 9 routinely contrasts galvanic vs electrolytic cells: in both, oxidation is anode, reduction is cathode вЂ” but the cell polarity and free-energy sign are flipped.

questions.prompt: Constant F = 96,485 used to convert charge to moles of electrons.
questions.explanation: Faraday's constant F = NA Г— e = (6.022 Г— 10ВІВі) Г— (1.602 Г— 10вЃ»В№вЃ№) = 96,485 C/mol вЂ” the charge of one mole of electrons. Named for Michael Faraday, whose 1834 electrolysis laws stated: (1) mass deposited is proportional to charge passed, and (2) for a given charge, mass scales with M/n (where n is electrons per ion). Combined: m = ItM/(nF), where I is current (A), t is time (s), M is molar mass, n is electrons per ion. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely uses this 'm = ItM/nF' equation for electroplating and electrolysis mass calculations вЂ” a near-annual fixture.

questions.prompt: Equation m = (I Г— t Г— M)/(n Г— F) for mass deposited during electrolysis.
questions.explanation: Faraday's law of electrolysis: mass deposited m = (I Г— t Г— M) / (n Г— F), where I is current in amps (A = C/s), t in seconds, M is molar mass in g/mol, n is electrons per ion (1 for AgвЃє/Ag; 2 for CuВІвЃє/Cu; 3 for AlВівЃє/Al), F = 96485 C/mol. Equivalent: moles deposited = (It) / (nF). Used industrially for electroplating thickness control (typically Вµm-level), electrorefining of copper (purity 99.999%), and aluminum production (each kg Al requires ~5 kg COв‚‚ from carbon anode oxidation). AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely supplies I, t, and asks for grams plated.

questions.prompt: Direction of cation movement in an electrolytic cell.
questions.explanation: In both galvanic and electrolytic cells, cations (positive ions) migrate toward the cathode (where reduction occurs and electrons accumulate), and anions toward the anode. The cathode (in electrolytic cells, the negative terminal) attracts positive ions for reduction; in galvanic cells (cathode is positive terminal of the cell, but still attracts cations internally because reduction consumes them). The mnemonic 'cations to cathode, anions to anode' helps. In electroplating, CuВІвЃє ions from solution migrate to the cathode where they are reduced and deposited as Cu metal. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely tests directional ion flow.

questions.prompt: Minimum voltage above EВ° required to overcome activation energy in an electrolytic cell.
questions.explanation: Overpotential (О·) is the additional voltage beyond the thermodynamic minimum (в€’EВ°_cell) needed to drive an electrolysis at a useful rate. It arises from kinetic barriers: charge transfer at electrodes, concentration polarization (depletion of ions near the surface), and gas evolution barriers (Hв‚‚ at the cathode in chlor-alkali requires ~0.4 V extra). Total cell voltage = |EВ°_cell| + ОЈО· + IR (resistance). Overpotential makes electrolysis less energy-efficient than the thermodynamic minimum predicts. AP Chemistry Unit 9 introduces overpotential conceptually; quantitative treatment is reserved for college-level electrochemistry, but recognition is rubric-relevant.

questions.prompt: Quantity equal to atomic number; identifies the element.
questions.explanation: The atomic number Z equals the number of protons in the nucleus and uniquely identifies the element. Carbon always has Z = 6; oxygen always has Z = 8. Mass number A = protons + neutrons, so neutrons = A в€’ Z. Isotopes share Z (same element, same chemistry) but differ in A (and thus neutron count). The number of electrons in a neutral atom equals Z; in an ion, it equals Z minus the charge. AP Chemistry Unit 1 expects fluency with these relationships from PES, mass-spec, and isotope-notation problems. Cumulative-yearlong reviews layer this on later topics like ion size and lattice energy.

questions.prompt: Strongest dipole-dipole IMF, occurring when H is bonded to F, O, or N.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds are the strongest IMF (5-30 kJ/mol), forming when H is covalently bonded to F, O, or N (small, very electronegative) and interacts with a lone pair on F, O, or N of another molecule. Crucial for water's anomalous BP, ice density, DNA structure, protein folding, and solubility of polar molecules in water. Substances that can't H-bond (e.g., CHв‚„) have much lower BP at similar molar mass: methane в€’161В°C vs water 100В°C. AP Chemistry Unit 3 demands accurate identification: NHв‚ѓ yes, but CHв‚ѓF no (despite F being electronegative, H is on C, not F).

questions.prompt: Geometry of Hв‚‚O around its central oxygen.
questions.explanation: Water's oxygen has four electron domains (2 bonding + 2 lone pairs), so the electron geometry is tetrahedral and the molecular geometry is bent. The H-O-H angle is ~104.5В°, smaller than the ideal tetrahedral 109.5В° because lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs. The bent shape, combined with the polar O-H bonds, gives water a substantial dipole (1.85 D), driving its anomalously high boiling point, surface tension, heat capacity, and density maximum at 4В°C. AP Chemistry connects Unit 2 geometry directly to Unit 3 IMF and Unit 6 thermodynamic anomalies.

questions.prompt: Element with electron configuration [Ar] 4sВІ 3dвЃ¶.
questions.explanation: Iron (Fe, Z = 26) has configuration 1sВІ 2sВІ 2pвЃ¶ 3sВІ 3pвЃ¶ 4sВІ 3dвЃ¶ = [Ar] 4sВІ 3dвЃ¶. Note 4sВІ before 3dвЃ¶ вЂ” for neutral atoms, 4s fills before 3d (lower energy due to penetration). For FeВІвЃє ion, electrons are removed from 4s FIRST, giving [Ar] 3dвЃ¶ вЂ” the 4s vs 3d removal order reversal is a near-annual AP Chemistry trap. Fe has four unpaired d electrons (Hund's rule), so it is paramagnetic вЂ” and FeВІвЃє is also paramagnetic. Iron's biological roles (hemoglobin, cytochromes, ferritin) make this Z = 26 special.

questions.prompt: Hybridization on the carbon in COв‚‚ molecule.
questions.explanation: In COв‚‚ (O=C=O), the central carbon has 2 electron domains (two C=O double bonds), so it is sp hybridized. The two sp orbitals lie 180В° apart (linear geometry). Each C=O double bond consists of 1 Пѓ (from sp + p overlap) + 1 ПЂ (from unhybridized 2p of C with unhybridized 2p of each O). So COв‚‚ has 2 Пѓ and 2 ПЂ bonds total. The linear geometry makes COв‚‚ nonpolar despite having polar bonds вЂ” bond dipoles cancel by symmetry. This makes COв‚‚ a poor solvent for ionic compounds but excellent at very high P (supercritical COв‚‚). AP Chemistry Unit 2 routinely uses COв‚‚.

questions.prompt: Quantity that the rate of a reaction has units of.
questions.explanation: Reaction rate has units of M/s (or mol/(LВ·s)) вЂ” concentration change per unit time. Rate is always positive and equal to в€’О”[reactant]/О”t Г— (1/coefficient) = О”[product]/О”t Г— (1/coefficient). For aA в†’ bB, rate = в€’(1/a)В·О”[A]/О”t = (1/b)В·О”[B]/О”t.
