The rate constant k has different units depending on order: zero order M/s; first order 1/s; second order 1/(MВ·s). Plug into rate = k[A]^m gives M/s on the left. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely tests rate units, especially for k вЂ” a frequent rubric point.

questions.prompt: Concentration unit that does not depend on temperature.
questions.explanation: Molality (m) is moles of solute per kilogram of solvent: m = n / m_solvent. Unlike molarity (mol/L solution), molality does NOT depend on temperature because mass вЂ” unlike volume вЂ” doesn't change with T. So molality is preferred for colligative-property equations: О”T_f = K_f В· m В· i, О”T_b = K_b В· m В· i, where i is the van't Hoff factor. Conversion: dilute aqueous solutions have m в‰€ M (since 1 L water в‰€ 1 kg), but for concentrated or non-aqueous solutions, conversion requires density. AP Chemistry Unit 3 routinely tests freezing-point-depression problems using molality.

questions.prompt: Equation: solubility = k Г— pressure for a gas in liquid.
questions.explanation: Henry's law: solubility of a gas in liquid = k_H Г— P, where k_H is the Henry's law constant and P is the partial pressure of that gas above the solution. As P rises (more gas pressed against the liquid surface), more gas dissolves. Opening a soda decreases COв‚‚ partial pressure above the liquid, so dissolved COв‚‚ rapidly comes out of solution as bubbles. SCUBA divers ascending too quickly experience decompression sickness as dissolved Nв‚‚ comes out of blood as bubbles. AP Chemistry Unit 3 routinely tests Henry's law in environmental (COв‚‚ in ocean), biological (Oв‚‚ in blood), and laboratory (gas-collection-over-water) contexts.

questions.prompt: Quantity in PV=nRT that has units of LВ·atm/(molВ·K) with value 0.0821.
questions.explanation: The ideal gas constant R = 0.0821 LВ·atm/(molВ·K) = 8.314 J/(molВ·K) = 8.314 PaВ·mВі/(molВ·K) = 62.36 LВ·torr/(molВ·K). Different forms are used in different contexts: 0.0821 for PV=nRT in LВ·atm; 8.314 for energy calculations (Arrhenius, Nernst, О”G = в€’RT ln K). Choosing the right R is a frequent AP Chemistry trap. AP Chemistry's official equation sheet supplies all relevant R values. R appears in PV=nRT (Unit 3), in Arrhenius (Unit 5), in О”G = в€’RT ln K (Units 6, 7, 9), and in the Nernst equation (Unit 9) вЂ” likely the most cross-unit constant on the exam.

questions.prompt: Process where О”H_sub = О”H_fus + this enthalpy by Hess's law.
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, sublimation (solid в†’ gas) = fusion (solid в†’ liquid) + vaporization (liquid в†’ gas), so О”H_sub = О”H_fus + О”H_vap. For water at 0В°C: О”H_fus = 6.01 kJ/mol; О”H_vap = 40.65 kJ/mol; О”H_sub в‰€ 46.7 kJ/mol. This makes sense вЂ” sublimation skips the liquid phase but requires the same total energy to go from rigidly bonded solid all the way to free-flying gas. AP Chemistry Units 3 and 6 routinely use this Hess's-law decomposition in phase-change problems and Born-Haber-style energy diagrams. Reverse processes have opposite-sign enthalpies.

questions.prompt: Sign of О”SВ° for a reaction that consumes 2 moles of gas to make 0 moles of gas.
questions.explanation: Reactions that decrease О”n_gas (fewer moles of gas after) have negative О”S, because gas has the highest entropy. Example: 2 NO(g) + Oв‚‚(g) в†’ 2 NOв‚‚(g) goes from 3 moles gas в†’ 2 moles gas, О”n = в€’1, О”SВ° < 0 (в‰€ в€’146 J/(molВ·K)). Even more dramatic: Nв‚‚(g) + 3 Hв‚‚(g) в†’ 2 NHв‚ѓ(g), О”n = в€’2, О”SВ° = в€’198.3 J/(molВ·K). Predicting О”S sign from О”n_gas alone is the rubric-rewarded approach. AP Chemistry Unit 6 routinely tests this prediction at the start of free-response, with quantitative О”SВ° calculation as the next-step extension.

questions.prompt: Effect on rate of doubling [A] when reaction is first-order in A.
questions.explanation: For a reaction first-order in A (rate = k[A]В№), doubling [A] doubles the rate (2В№ = 2). Second-order: doubling [A] quadruples rate (2ВІ = 4). Zero-order: doubling [A] has no effect (2вЃ° = 1). This is how the method of initial rates determines order experimentally вЂ” compare rates between trials where [A] is doubled while other concentrations are held constant. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely tests order-determination using doubling ratios, then computes the rate constant k by plugging one trial into rate = k[A]^m[B]^n.

questions.prompt: Substance regenerated at the end of a reaction mechanism that speeds the reaction.
questions.explanation: A catalyst is consumed in one elementary step and regenerated in a later step, so it does not appear in the overall balanced equation. By providing an alternative mechanism with lower Ea, catalysts speed both forward and reverse reactions equally вЂ” they do NOT change K, О”G, О”H, О”S, or product yield. Homogeneous catalysts (e.g., aqueous HвЃє) share the reactant phase; heterogeneous (e.g., Pt in catalytic converters) do not. Enzymes are biological catalysts, often providing 10вЃ¶ to 10В№ВІ rate enhancement. AP Chemistry Units 5 and 7 routinely test catalyst recognition and the K-unchanged conceptual point.

questions.prompt: Cumulative О”H for a reaction via products' О”Hf minus reactants' О”Hf.
questions.explanation: О”HВ°_rxn = ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(products) в€’ ОЈОЅВ·О”HВ°_f(reactants), where ОЅ is the stoichiometric coefficient. This Hess-derived formula uses tabulated formation enthalpies. Elements in their standard states have О”HВ°_f = 0 by definition. Example: CHв‚„ + 2 Oв‚‚ в†’ COв‚‚ + 2 Hв‚‚O: О”H = [(-393.5) + 2(-285.8)] - [(-74.8) + 2(0)] = -965.1 + 74.8 = -890.3 kJ/mol. Matches experimental в€’890 kJ/mol for methane combustion. AP Chemistry Unit 6 free-response routinely provides a О”HВ°_f table and asks for О”HВ°_rxn вЂ” a near-annual 2-point question.

questions.prompt: Order n in a reaction whose t_ВЅ is independent of starting [A].
questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, t_ВЅ = ln(2)/k = 0.693/k вЂ” independent of starting [A]. This is the unique signature of first-order kinetics, exploited in radioactive-decay applications: В№вЃґC dating works because all radionuclides decay first-order with constant t_ВЅ (5730 yr for В№вЃґC, 4.47 Г— 10вЃ№ yr for ВІВівЃёU). For zero-order, t_ВЅ = [A]в‚Ђ/(2k) вЂ” proportional to [A]в‚Ђ. For second-order, t_ВЅ = 1/(k[A]в‚Ђ) вЂ” inverse to [A]в‚Ђ. Plotting t_ВЅ at different starting concentrations is one way to identify reaction order; the AP Chemistry Unit 5 alternate uses graphs of [A], ln[A], or 1/[A] vs t.

questions.prompt: Effect of catalyst on the value of K.
questions.explanation: Catalysts speed both forward and reverse reactions equally вЂ” they lower Ea_forward AND Ea_reverse by the same amount, so the ratio k_f/k_r is unchanged. K = k_f/k_r is therefore unchanged. Also unchanged: О”GВ°, О”HВ°, О”SВ°, equilibrium concentrations. Catalysts let a reaction REACH equilibrium faster but never SHIFT the equilibrium position. Haber-Bosch needs high P (to shift Le Chatelier toward NHв‚ѓ) AND a catalyst (to make the reaction fast enough at moderate T) вЂ” both are necessary because they accomplish different things. AP Chemistry Units 5 and 7 routinely test this nuanced point.

questions.prompt: Sign of О”G when a reaction is at equilibrium.
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, О”G = 0 (no net driving force in either direction). This is true for the actual О”G, not necessarily О”GВ° (which is at standard conditions, 1 M or 1 atm вЂ” not the equilibrium state unless K happens to equal 1). Setting О”G = О”GВ° + RT ln Q = 0 at equilibrium (Q = K) recovers О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K. So О”G = 0 is the universal equilibrium condition, while О”GВ° depends on tabulated values. AP Chemistry Units 6 and 7 demand fluency: 'at equilibrium О”G = 0, which means Q = K and the position is fixed.' This is a frequent rubric point.

questions.prompt: Increasing temperature shifts K toward this for an exothermic forward reaction.
questions.explanation: For an exothermic forward reaction (О”H < 0), increasing T shifts equilibrium backward (Le Chatelier: heat is a product; raising T pushes back), so K decreases. Mathematically, the van't Hoff equation ln(Kв‚‚/Kв‚Ѓ) = в€’(О”HВ°/R)(1/Tв‚‚ в€’ 1/Tв‚Ѓ) gives negative ln ratio when О”H < 0 and Tв‚‚ > Tв‚Ѓ, confirming Kв‚‚ < Kв‚Ѓ. For endothermic, the opposite holds. AP Chemistry Unit 7 free-response routinely tests T-K relationship: 'how does K change when T is raised for this reaction?' The answer depends on the sign of О”H вЂ” students must compute О”H first if not given.

questions.prompt: Value of О”GВ° when K = 1 at any temperature.
questions.explanation: О”GВ° = в€’RT ln K. When K = 1, ln K = 0, so О”GВ° = 0 at any temperature. This is a special case: products and reactants are equally favored at standard state. It corresponds to a reaction whose О”HВ° equals TО”SВ° at the experimental T (where the two driving forces exactly cancel). Example: a phase transition at its equilibrium T has О”G = 0 (e.g., water boiling at 100В°C, 1 atm has О”G = 0 because the system is at equilibrium with vapor). AP Chemistry Units 6 and 7 connect this idea: О”G = 0 в†” Q = K, but О”GВ° = 0 в†” K = 1 specifically.

questions.prompt: Charge ratio for a multi-step problem: 96485 C/mol times n times this gives moles plated.
questions.explanation: Faraday's law of electrolysis: moles deposited = (I Г— t) / (n Г— F), where I is current (A), t is time (s), n is electrons per ion, F = 96485 C/mol. Example: electroplating Cu (n=2) with I = 2.0 A for t = 3600 s gives moles = (2.0 Г— 3600) / (2 Г— 96485) = 0.0373 mol; mass = 0.0373 Г— 63.55 = 2.37 g Cu. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely tests Faraday's law in industrial-electrolysis (aluminum production, chlor-alkali process) and electroplating contexts. Rubrics demand correct n value and correct R/F constant вЂ” frequent unit-error trap.

questions.prompt: PH of a 0.0010 M HCl solution.
questions.explanation: HCl is a strong acid (full dissociation): [HвЃє] = 0.0010 M = 10вЃ»Ві M, so pH = в€’log(10вЃ»Ві) = 3.0. Diluting strong acid 10Г— raises pH by exactly 1 unit. For strong-acid concentrations below ~10вЃ»вЃ¶ M, water's autoionization becomes significant вЂ” at 10вЃ»вЃ· M HCl, water contributes more HвЃє than the acid, and pH в‰€ 6.79 (not 7.0). AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely computes strong-acid pH directly and contrasts with weak-acid pH, which is always higher (less acidic) at the same nominal concentration.

questions.prompt: Indicator color change pH range that straddles the equivalence of strong acid + weak base.
questions.explanation: When titrating a weak base (e.g., NHв‚ѓ) with a strong acid (e.g., HCl), the equivalence-point solution contains only NHв‚„вЃє (the conjugate acid) and ClвЃ». NHв‚„вЃє hydrolyzes water to give some HвЃє, so equivalence pH < 7 (typically 5-6 for ~0.1 M solutions). The indicator must change color in this acidic range вЂ” methyl orange (pKa в‰€ 3.7, color change 3.1-4.4) or methyl red (pKa в‰€ 5.0) are good choices. Phenolphthalein (color change at pH 8.3-10) is wrong because it changes color too late, missing the equivalence point. AP Chemistry Unit 8 tests this indicator-selection logic routinely.

questions.prompt: Equation for cell potential at non-standard concentrations.
questions.explanation: E = EВ° в€’ (RT/nF) ln Q (or at 25В°C, E = EВ° в€’ (0.0592/n) log Q). As products accumulate (Q rises), the (0.0592/n) log Q term grows, dragging E down from EВ°. At Q = K, E = 0 (battery dead). Concentration cells use this principle: same redox couple in two compartments, different ion concentrations, generates voltage from Q в‰  1 alone. AP Chemistry Unit 9 free-response routinely applies the Nernst equation to non-standard cells and concentration cells, and the equation appears on the official AP equation sheet for use during the exam.

questions.prompt: Mole ratio in a buffer where pH equals pKa.
questions.explanation: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([AвЃ»]/[HA]). When [AвЃ»]/[HA] = 1 (equal moles), log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa. This is the half-equivalence point of a weak-acid titration. Buffer capacity is highest near 1:1 ratio (equal capacity to neutralize added acid or base). AP Chemistry Unit 8 free-response routinely uses Henderson-Hasselbalch in two directions: (1) given mole ratio, predict buffer pH; (2) given target pH, compute the required mole ratio. For pH within В±1 of pKa, the ratio range is approximately 1:10 to 10:1.

questions.prompt: Element with PES peaks at 273, 16.6, 11.5, and 1.51 MJ/mol in ratio 2:2:6:1.
questions.explanation: Sodium (Z = 11) has configuration 1sВІ 2sВІ 2pвЃ¶ 3sВ№, predicting four PES peaks with electron counts 2:2:6:1. The 1s appears at the highest binding energy (~273 MJ/mol вЂ” tightly bound innermost electrons), 2s next (~16.6 MJ/mol), 2p (~11.5 MJ/mol), and the lone 3s valence electron lowest (~1.51 MJ/mol). The peak-ratio match plus binding-energy magnitudes uniquely identify Na. AP Chemistry Unit 1 free-response Q4 in 2017 was almost exactly this prompt вЂ” students had to identify Na, write its configuration, and justify peak heights using subshell-occupancy logic. PES identification problems are a near-annual fixture and reward students who memorize peak-ratio patterns for common elements (Li 2:1; B 2:2:1; C 2:2:2; N 2:2:3; F 2:2:5; Na 2:2:6:1).
